title: SEGMENT 15 — The Takahashi macular degeneration trial: the first human iPSC

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — The Takahashi macular degeneration trial: the first human iPSC
Now let's talk about the very first clinical use of iPSC-derived cells in a human patient.

Because in 2014, before VX-880 was even close, before most clinical iPSC programs had started, a Japanese ophthalmologist named Masayo Takahashi — no relation to Kazutoshi Takahashi, Yamanaka's postdoc — did the experiment that put iPSCs into a human eye for the first time.
The disease was age-related macular degeneration, AMD.
AMD is the leading cause of blindness in elderly people in the developed world.
The disease involves dysfunction and death of the retinal pigment epithelium, or RPE — a single layer of pigmented cells that sit behind the photoreceptors and provide critical metabolic support.
When the RPE dies, the photoreceptors above it die, and vision is lost in the center of the visual field.
Replacing dead RPE with new RPE seemed, in principle, a tractable problem.
RPE is a single sheet of cells.
You don't have to integrate them into a complex circuit; you just have to put them in the right anatomical location.
And iPSC-derived RPE differentiation, by the early 2010s, was actually pretty well worked out.
The cells are highly distinctive — pigmented, polygonal, polarized — and you can verify identity just by looking at them.

Takahashi, working at the Riken Center for Developmental Biology, designed a trial in which she would take a skin biopsy from a patient with AMD, reprogram those fibroblasts to iPSCs, differentiate the iPSCs to RPE, manufacture a small sheet of RPE cells, and surgically transplant that sheet under the patient's retina to replace dying RPE.
Autologous, patient-specific iPSC therapy.
The first ever in humans.
The first patient was treated in September 2014.
The surgery was done by a colleague, Yasuo Kurimoto.
The patient was a 77-year-old woman from Hyogo prefecture.
Her own skin was reprogrammed, her own RPE was made, her own cells were transplanted into her own eye.
The procedure went well.
The cells engrafted.
Her vision stabilized — meaning, importantly, that the disease stopped progressing, even though the lost vision didn't return.
That was, in some ways, the more important result.
iPSC therapy was shown to be safe — no tumors, no aberrant differentiation, no obvious adverse effects from the cells themselves over years of follow-up.
The cells did their job.
The principle of patient-specific reprogramming-to-therapy had been demonstrated, end to end, in a real human being.
The trial then ran into a complication that illustrates one of the practical headaches of autologous iPSCs.
The second patient, in the pipeline, had iPSCs that — on careful sequencing — turned out to contain some mutations that hadn't been present in the starting fibroblasts.
The mutations were probably introduced during the reprogramming or culture process.
They weren't in cancer driver genes, and there was no obvious safety concern, but the regulators sensibly demanded explanation.
The trial was paused.
The team had to re-evaluate protocols.

Some of the wind went out of the autologous-iPSC sail, and the field began shifting more aggressively toward allogeneic approaches where you could thoroughly characterize one line and use it for many patients.
But the Takahashi trial remains historically momentous.
It was the proof that iPSCs could go all the way from skin biopsy to human therapy and back out with a safety record.
The pipeline existed.
The technology was real.
The regulatory framework, however cautiously, could accommodate it.
Since then, dozens of iPSC-based clinical trials have been launched around the world — for Parkinson's, for spinal cord injury, for heart failure, for blood disorders.
Some use autologous cells.
Most, increasingly, use allogeneic.
The Takahashi trial opened the door.
The rest of the field is walking through it.
